title: Geriatric Procedural Sedation – Increased Sensitivity and Comorbidities
description: An 82-year-old patient with multiple comorbidities requires moderate sedation for a colonoscopy. Practise recognising altered pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in elderly patients, conservative dosing, and management of geriatric-specific complications including delirium.
learningObjectives: Explain how age-related physiology alters drug PK/PD in elderly patients
Apply the 'start low, go slow' principle for geriatric sedation
Recognise and manage emergence delirium in elderly patients
Identify drug interactions relevant to polypharmacy in geriatric patients
Select the safest sedation agents for frail elderly patients

states.prompt: Your patient is an 82-year-old female (45 kg, BMI 21). PMH: atrial fibrillation (on warfarin), hypertension, mild cognitive impairment, osteoporosis, CKD stage 3. Current medications: warfarin, metoprolol, lisinopril, amlodipine, sertraline. Which physiological change MOST increases midazolam drug effect in elderly patients?

options.label: Increased central nervous system sensitivity (pharmacodynamic change)
Decreased renal clearance shortens drug half-life
Decreased muscle mass reduces volume of distribution
Faster hepatic metabolism increases drug effect

explanations: Correct! The dominant pharmacodynamic change in elderly patients is increased CNS receptor sensitivity (GABA-A upregulation, reduced neuronal reserves). The brain responds more strongly to the same drug concentration.
Decreased renal clearance PROLONGS benzodiazepine active metabolite elimination (extending duration), not shortens it.
Decreased muscle mass reduces lean Vd, increasing peak plasma levels — this is a PK effect, but the dominant concern is CNS PD hypersensitivity.
Hepatic metabolism DECREASES with age (reduced blood flow, phase I reactions), extending drug half-life.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies CNS PD hypersensitivity as dominant elderly drug effect mechanism

states.prompt: Time to sedate this 82-year-old for colonoscopy. Applying 'start low, go slow', what is the most appropriate INITIAL midazolam dose?

options.label: 0.5 mg IV (half the healthy adult starting dose)
1 mg IV (standard adult starting dose)
2 mg IV (front-load to ensure adequate sedation)
0.25 mg IV (below therapeutic threshold)

explanations: Correct! For patients >65 years (and especially >80), start at 0.5 mg IV (or lower) with slow titration. Geriatric PD hypersensitivity means 0.5 mg may be equivalent to 1–1.5 mg in a young adult.
1 mg is the standard adult dose but risks over-sedation, prolonged recovery, and post-procedure delirium in an 82-year-old with cognitive impairment.
2 mg is inappropriate — significant risk of apnoea, hypotension, and delirium in a frail elderly patient.
0.25 mg is at the lower limit but may be reasonable in very frail patients. For this 45 kg, cognitively impaired patient, 0.5 mg is the better validated starting point.

checklistItems.description: Selects 0.5 mg initial midazolam dose for geriatric patient

states.prompt: This patient is on sertraline (SSRI) and metoprolol. She is also receiving fentanyl 25 mcg for analgesia. Which drug interaction is MOST clinically relevant to monitor?

options.label: Fentanyl + sertraline: risk of serotonin syndrome (particularly with high fentanyl doses)
Metoprolol + midazolam: severe bradycardia from combined β-blockade and sedation
Warfarin + fentanyl: enhanced anticoagulation causing bleeding
Lisinopril + propofol: hypertensive emergency

explanations: Correct! Fentanyl (weak serotonergic activity) + sertraline (SSRI) can precipitate serotonin syndrome, especially at higher doses. Monitor for: agitation, tremor, clonus, hyperthermia, tachycardia.
Metoprolol does not significantly interact with midazolam pharmacodynamically for bradycardia. Monitoring is appropriate but not the primary risk.
Warfarin prolongs bleeding time but does not pharmacokinetically interact with fentanyl to enhance anticoagulation.
Propofol + ACE inhibitors do not cause hypertension — if anything, propofol causes vasodilation/hypotension.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies fentanyl + SSRI serotonin syndrome risk

states.prompt: Procedure complete. During recovery, the patient is agitated, disoriented, pulling at the IV, and calling out confused names. She does not recognise the nurse. This started 20 minutes post-procedure. MOASS 4 (eyes open, following commands inconsistently). What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.label: Post-procedure delirium (emergence delirium / PACU delirium)
Uncontrolled pain causing agitation
Hypoxia – SpO2 is 96% on 2 L/min NC
Paradoxical midazolam reaction

explanations: Correct! Emergence delirium in elderly patients with pre-existing cognitive impairment is a major post-sedation complication. Risk factors: age >65, pre-existing dementia, benzodiazepine use, sleep deprivation.
Pain can cause agitation but disorientation and failure to recognise staff point to delirium rather than isolated pain.
SpO2 96% is acceptable – hypoxia is not the primary cause here.
Paradoxical midazolam reaction typically occurs DURING sedation (acute combativeness), not 20 minutes into recovery.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies post-procedure delirium in elderly patient

states.prompt: The patient has post-procedure delirium. She is not violent but is confused and at risk of falls. What is the SAFEST first-line management?

options.label: Non-pharmacological: reorientation, familiar voice/family, low lighting, calm environment
Haloperidol 2 mg IV immediately to sedate the patient
Additional midazolam 1 mg IV to calm the agitation
Physical restraints to prevent falls

explanations: Correct! Non-pharmacological de-escalation is first-line for PACU delirium. Reorientation, familiar family presence, low-stimulus environment, and pain/urinary retention exclusion resolve most cases.
Haloperidol has a role in severe agitated delirium but carries QTc prolongation risk and should not be the reflex first-line in a non-violent patient.
Additional benzodiazepines WORSEN delirium and are contraindicated in elderly PACU delirium management.
Physical restraints increase delirium severity and injury risk. They are a last resort, not first-line.

checklistItems.description: Correctly selects non-pharmacological de-escalation for PACU delirium

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Elderly PD hypersensitivity: CNS responds more strongly at same plasma levels
• 'Start low, go slow': initial midazolam 0.5 mg (or less) for patients >65 years
• SSRI + fentanyl: monitor for serotonin syndrome
• Post-procedure delirium: non-pharmacological first; avoid benzodiazepines
